titleStmt.title: Letter from Molly Duffin to Maria Duffin, 4 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Molly Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.
This chatty letter from Molly to her mother Maria is mainly "ward gossip" and thanks for Christmas presents

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

correspAction.placeName: 1st Southern Gen. Hospital, Dudley Road, Birmingham, England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
1st Southern Gen. Hospital.
Dudley Road Jan. 4th /16 Dearest Mamma, I tried to get into bed early to-night to
write some letters, but there is always a scuffle for
baths before settling in, and the Sisters on the next
corridor will come and use our bathroom although they
have one of their own and they have no consciences about
keeping you waiting for hours! Of course there are
exceptions, but I think the whole race of Sisters are very
disagreeable, and almost more so when they are off—duty! I am still in the same ward with my nice Sister
in charge, but the Nursing Sister is a horrid little
thing and I am hoping she will be moved out first;
she has never been in a military hospital before and
I am sure she doesn't understand how to deal with
these wounds that are nearly all septic and with very
often with contaminated fractures underneath. She handles
them so roughly, the men all dread her coming near
them and the other day when one of them was coming
out of chloroform he shouted out such rude things
about her, it was quite embarrassing. She is always
grumbling about how she is treated here, so I hope
she will soon return to wherever she came from! We had another whist drive in my ward on New
Year's Day with a supper afterwards, when Sister
Williams was presented with a beautiful manicure set
from the patients and the VAD's with nice little books.
Mine is a little leather one of Moore's Melodies — Wasn't
it nice of them? Unfortunately in the middle of the
whist drive, one of the men's legs began to haemorrhage
and we had to rush off for the doctor etc. which
rather broke up the evening; however, he was able to
stop it alright. That is the only thing I am
afraid of, as of course a minute or two wasted may
just make all the difference and I never much like
being left alone on duty in a surgical ward. Sister Hodgkinson departed 3/4 of an hour before her
time this afternoon as she said she wanted to catch a
train and left me in charge of the ward. The Matron
came round on an inspection, but fortunately came in
just before Sister left, as of curse it is quite against
D2109/10/1/A 3
the rules. I like the Mr. E. T Irwin book
you gave me very much. Did you read it, I wonder?
It is very original and rather the same style as
Come out to Play There don't seem to be many
new novels this year. I am going to the theatre
with Mrs Chance, R White's sister, next week. I think
to see The Mikado as the D'Oyley Carte Company is
here. Please thank Helen for exchanging
the silk blouse which arrived this evening and fits
very well. It is nice to have a new one, as the
others were shabby, also the new gloves, which I am
so pleased to have. I nearly bought a new pair the
other day, but luckily put it off! Ever your loving
Molly
